title: What Is Bear Market? Definition, Formula & Examples
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: What is Bear Market? Learn the definition, formula, and real-world examples of Bear Market. Our comprehensive guide explains how Bear Market works and why it matters for investors.
excerpt: A bear market occurs when an asset or index falls 20% or more from its recent peak, typically accompanied by widespread pessimism, declining corporate earnings, and economic contraction. Bear markets ...

content.definition: A bear market occurs when an asset or index falls 20% or more from its recent peak, typically accompanied by widespread pessimism, declining corporate earnings, and economic contraction. Bear markets are a normal part of market cycles, occurring roughly every 5-7 years on average. While painful, they historically present excellent buying opportunities for long-term investors, as markets have always eventually recovered and reached new highs.
content.formula: Bear Market Threshold = Most Recent Peak × 0.80 (a 20% decline confirms a bear market)
content.example: In 2022, the S&P 500 peaked at 4,797 in January and fell to 3,577 by October — a decline of 25.4%, officially entering bear market territory. Investors who stayed the course saw the market recover fully within 18 months.
content.types: Secular bear markets are prolonged (10+ years) with fundamentally weak economics. Cyclical bear markets last months to 1-2 years within broader uptrends. A "correction" is a 10-20% decline — less severe but more common, occurring about once per year on average.
content.whyItMatters: Understanding Bear Market is essential for making informed investment decisions. Whether you are evaluating individual stocks, planning your portfolio allocation, or comparing investment options, Bear Market provides a quantitative framework that removes guesswork and helps you assess value objectively. Professional investors, analysts, and financial advisors all rely on Bear Market as a core metric in their decision-making process.
